task: HyMem and Dual-Layer Memory Architecture Research

hymem.title: HyMem: Hybrid Memory Architecture with Dynamic Retrieval Scheduling
hymem.key_idea: Do not choose between compression and retention — maintain both L1 summaries and L2 raw text, then route queries dynamically to the appropriate granularity level.

storage.L1: Event-level summaries (context, time, location, participants, general progression) — fast retrieval
storage.L2: Raw dialogue text per event — full fidelity
storage.linking: Explicit L1→L2 pointers for backtracking without re-processing

retrieval.tier1_lightweight: Cosine similarity over L1 summaries. Generates answer from L1, evaluates completeness. Always activated.
retrieval.tier2_deep: Activated only when T1 detects incompleteness (~22% of queries at k=10). Coarse semantic recall → LLM self-retrieval → L2 raw text backtracking.
retrieval.reflection: If query still unresolved after T2, decomposes into sub-queries for multi-hop chains.

architecture.write_policy: Static at ingest — events summarized at write time, no background consolidation.

strengths: SOTA on LOCOMO and LongMemEval-s simultaneously
92.6% token reduction while beating full-context accuracy
Tier 2 only activates 22% of the time — very efficient
Reflection mechanism handles multi-hop and causal chains robustly
Elegant L1→L2 backtracking without re-summarization cost
weaknesses: No write-time consolidation — stored events are static, no knowledge evolution
Completeness detection heuristic in T1 is a critical single point of failure
L1 summary quality entirely dependent on initial event segmentation
No cross-session knowledge evolution (unlike A-MEM or HiMem reconsolidation)
NOT a true episodic/semantic split — lacks 'what it means' abstraction layer

episodic.definition: What happened — specific events, interactions, tool calls, outcomes with temporal grounding
episodic.retrieval: Deterministic window queries (last N events, session scope, timestamp filters)

semantic.definition: What it means — distilled facts, preferences, profiles, timeless generalized knowledge
semantic.write_policy: Selective, expensive (LLM distillation required), background async
semantic.retrieval: k-NN vector similarity + BM25 hybrid search

HiMem.pattern: Immutable episodes + evolving Note Memory with reconsolidation (ADD/UPDATE/DELETE)

Synapse.pattern: Unified graph with episodic+semantic nodes, spreading activation retrieval
Synapse.retrieval: BM25+embeddings → fan effect → lateral inhibition → sigmoid normalization
Synapse.benchmark: LOCOMO F1 40.5 (+7.2 vs A-MEM), token cost 814/query

SimpleMem.pattern: Entropy-gated ingest (τ=0.35) + recursive consolidation (τ=0.85 affinity) + adaptive k
SimpleMem.benchmark: 30x token reduction vs Mem0; ablation: -31.3% multi-hop without consolidation

A_MEM.pattern: Zettelkasten linked notes, every write triggers link creation and note refinement, no separate episodic

Mem0.pattern: Extract-then-Update pipeline (two-phase: extract candidates, reconcile with existing)
Mem0.benchmark: 26% relative improvement over OpenAI memory on LOCOMO (66.9% vs 52.9%), 90% token reduction

MemGPT.pattern: Core/Recall/Archival memory, agent self-edits via tool calls — foundational OS analogy

evidence: HiMem vs A-MEM on LOCOMO: +28.83 pp
SimpleMem consolidation ablation: -31.3% multi-hop without it
why_dual_beats_flat.note_on_40pct_claim: No single paper makes an exact '40% improvement' claim. Closest: HyMem multi-hop +41.3 pp (88.16% vs 46.89%). The ~40% figure likely refers to this result or aggregate multi-hop gains across papers.
structural_reasons: Recency vs. relevance: episodic handles recency, semantic handles topical relevance — no tradeoff
Scale: episodic window bounded, semantic cache amortizes cost across queries
Multi-hop: reflection mechanism decomposes complex queries into sub-problems
Generalization: semantic layer consolidates cross-session patterns into reusable knowledge
Auditability: episodic provides reproducible event timeline with provenance
Cost: token usage sublinear vs history length (vs linear for full-context)

tables.episodes: id, session_id, timestamp, event_type, content, embedding, ttl_expiry
tables.semantic: id, source_episode_ids (provenance), content, embedding, bm25_index, tags, confidence, last_updated

write_policy.episodic: Append-only on every event, no LLM at write time
write_policy.semantic: Async background job. Cluster by embedding similarity (τ=0.85) + temporal proximity. LLM synthesizes clusters. HiMem ADD/UPDATE/DELETE reconciliation.
write_policy.dedup: fnv1a_hash for exact dedup; embedding similarity >0.90 for semantic dedup

retrieval.strategy: Run episodic (last N=10 or session window, deterministic) and semantic (top-k=5, hybrid vector+BM25) in PARALLEL
retrieval.merge: Dedup by source, apply ~3K token budget, conflict rule: freshest episodic wins over semantic
retrieval.lancedb_native: Use LanceDB's native hybrid search (vector+BM25) for semantic table; timestamp-filtered scans for episodic (prefer auditability over vector search)

consolidation.timing: Background async — NOT on the hot retrieval path
consolidation.algorithm: SimpleMem τ=0.85 affinity clustering → LLM synthesis → HiMem reconciliation (ADD/UPDATE/DELETE)

ttl.semantic: No TTL; confidence decay (Ebbinghaus-inspired) based on access frequency
